title: Recoding Variables with mutate()
description: Learn to recode and transform variables using mutate() with case_when() for range-based conditions and recode() for value replacement, across three real ggplot2 datasets.

tasks.title: case_when(): categorising sleep duration
tasks.instructions:
`case_when()` lets you create a new column based on a series of conditions — think of it as a vectorised `if / else if / else`.

The `msleep` dataset (built into ggplot2) records sleep times for 83 mammals.

Using `mutate()` and `case_when()`, add a column called `sleep_cat` to `msleep`:
- `"short"` — `sleep_total` less than 8 hours
- `"average"` — `sleep_total` between 8 and 12 hours (inclusive)
- `"long"` — `sleep_total` more than 12 hours

Save the result as `msleep2` and print a count of each category.
tasks.starter_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

msleep2 <- mutate(msleep,
  sleep_cat = case_when(
    sleep_total < 8   ~ "short",
    sleep_total <= 12 ~ ________,
    sleep_total > 12  ~ ________
  )
)

count(msleep2, sleep_cat)
tasks.solution_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

msleep2 <- mutate(msleep,
  sleep_cat = case_when(
    sleep_total < 8   ~ "short",
    sleep_total <= 12 ~ "average",
    sleep_total > 12  ~ "long"
  )
)

count(msleep2, sleep_cat)
tasks.test_expression: exists('msleep2') && 'sleep_cat' %in% names(msleep2) && all(c('short','average','long') %in% msleep2$sleep_cat) && all(msleep2$sleep_cat[!is.na(msleep2$sleep_total) & msleep2$sleep_total < 8] == 'short', na.rm=TRUE) && all(msleep2$sleep_cat[!is.na(msleep2$sleep_total) & msleep2$sleep_total > 12] == 'long', na.rm=TRUE)
hints: case_when() evaluates conditions in order — the first TRUE condition wins.
Each condition uses the syntax: condition ~ "value".
The three conditions cover < 8, between 8 and 12, and > 12. Make sure the boundaries don't leave any gap.
sleep_total <= 12 ~ "average", sleep_total > 12 ~ "long"
tasks.ai_context: Task introduces case_when() for range-based recoding. The boundaries matter: < 8 for short, <= 12 for average, > 12 for long. Some animals have NA for sleep_total — these will produce NA in sleep_cat, which is correct behaviour. Encourage students to notice the NAs in the count output.

tasks.title: recode(): replacing values one-to-one
tasks.instructions:
`recode()` is the tidyverse tool for straightforward value substitution — replacing specific values with new ones without needing to write conditions.

The `midwest` dataset records demographic data for counties across five US states. The `state` column contains abbreviations: `"IL"`, `"IN"`, `"MI"`, `"OH"`, `"WI"`.

Using `mutate()` and `recode()`, add a column `state_name` that replaces each abbreviation with the full state name. Save as `midwest2`.

*Syntax:* `recode(column, "old" = "new", ...)`
tasks.starter_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

midwest2 <- mutate(midwest,
  state_name = recode(state,
    "IL" = "Illinois",
    "IN" = ________,
    "MI" = ________,
    "OH" = ________,
    "WI" = ________
  )
)

count(midwest2, state, state_name)
tasks.solution_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

midwest2 <- mutate(midwest,
  state_name = recode(state,
    "IL" = "Illinois",
    "IN" = "Indiana",
    "MI" = "Michigan",
    "OH" = "Ohio",
    "WI" = "Wisconsin"
  )
)

count(midwest2, state, state_name)
hints: recode() takes the column as its first argument, then named pairs of old = "new" values.
The five states are Illinois, Indiana, Michigan, Ohio, and Wisconsin.
Each pair looks like: "IN" = "Indiana"
tasks.ai_context: Task introduces recode() for one-to-one value replacement. The five states are IL=Illinois, IN=Indiana, MI=Michigan, OH=Ohio, WI=Wisconsin. The count(midwest2, state, state_name) at the end is a nice way to verify every abbreviation mapped correctly.

tasks.title: case_when(): price tiers in diamonds
tasks.instructions:
The `diamonds` dataset contains prices and characteristics of ~54,000 diamonds.

Using `mutate()` and `case_when()`, create a `price_tier` column:
- `"budget"` — price under $1,000
- `"mid-range"` — price $1,000 to $4,999
- `"premium"` — price $5,000 or more

Save as `diamonds2`. Then use `count()` to see how many diamonds fall in each tier.

This time the starter code is more sparse — fill in the conditions yourself.
tasks.starter_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

diamonds2 <- mutate(diamonds,
  price_tier = case_when(

  )
)

count(diamonds2, price_tier)
tasks.solution_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

diamonds2 <- mutate(diamonds,
  price_tier = case_when(
    price < 1000 ~ "budget",
    price < 5000 ~ "mid-range",
    TRUE         ~ "premium"
  )
)

count(diamonds2, price_tier)
tasks.test_expression: exists('diamonds2') && 'price_tier' %in% names(diamonds2) && all(c('budget','mid-range','premium') %in% diamonds2$price_tier) && all(diamonds2$price_tier[diamonds2$price < 1000] == 'budget') && all(diamonds2$price_tier[diamonds2$price >= 5000] == 'premium')
hints: case_when() checks conditions in order — once a row matches, the remaining conditions are skipped.
Because conditions are checked in order, price < 5000 on the second line only catches rows that didn't already match price < 1000.
TRUE ~ "premium" as the final line acts as a catch-all — any row not matched above gets "premium".
case_when(price < 1000 ~ "budget", price < 5000 ~ "mid-range", TRUE ~ "premium")
tasks.ai_context: Key insight: because case_when() evaluates in order, you don't need to write price >= 1000 & price < 5000 for the middle tier — price < 5000 alone is sufficient since rows under 1000 are already handled. TRUE ~ "premium" as a catch-all is the idiomatic pattern. Encourage students to try writing it the long way first and see that both work.

tasks.title: recode(): many-to-one colour grouping
tasks.instructions:
Diamonds are graded for colour on a scale from D (colourless) to J (faint yellow). The `color` column in `diamonds` uses these GIA grades.

Use `recode()` to collapse the seven grades into three broader groups in a new column `color_group`:
- `"colorless"` — grades D, E, F
- `"near-colorless"` — grades G, H
- `"faint"` — grades I, J

This is a **many-to-one** recode: multiple input values map to the same output. Save as `diamonds3`.

The starter code is intentionally sparse this time.
tasks.starter_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

diamonds3 <- mutate(diamonds,
  color_group = recode(as.character(color),

  )
)

count(diamonds3, color, color_group)
tasks.solution_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

diamonds3 <- mutate(diamonds,
  color_group = recode(as.character(color),
    "D" = "colorless",
    "E" = "colorless",
    "F" = "colorless",
    "G" = "near-colorless",
    "H" = "near-colorless",
    "I" = "faint",
    "J" = "faint"
  )
)

count(diamonds3, color, color_group)
hints: recode() maps each old value to a new one. You can map multiple old values to the same new value.
color is a factor in diamonds — as.character(color) converts it to a plain character vector that recode() handles more predictably.
You need seven entries in recode(), one per grade: D, E, F, G, H, I, J.
"D" = "colorless", "E" = "colorless", "F" = "colorless", "G" = "near-colorless", ...
tasks.ai_context: Key teaching point: recode() handles many-to-one by simply repeating the same output value. The as.character() wrapper is needed because color is an ordered factor and recode() behaves more predictably with plain character input. The count(diamonds3, color, color_group) output is a good sanity check — students can see all 7 grades mapped to 3 groups.

tasks.title: case_when(): combining two conditions
tasks.instructions:
`case_when()` really shines when you need to cross multiple variables to create a category.

Using the `midwest` dataset, create a `county_type` column that combines urban status (`inmetro`: 1 = metro, 0 = non-metro) with poverty level (`percbelowpoverty`):

- `inmetro == 1` and `percbelowpoverty <= 15` → `"urban-low-poverty"`
- `inmetro == 1` and `percbelowpoverty > 15` → `"urban-high-poverty"`
- `inmetro == 0` and `percbelowpoverty <= 15` → `"rural-low-poverty"`
- `inmetro == 0` and `percbelowpoverty > 15` → `"rural-high-poverty"`

Save as `midwest3`. The starter code gives you the shell only — the `mutate()` call is yours to write.
tasks.starter_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

midwest3 <- mutate(midwest,

)

count(midwest3, county_type)
tasks.solution_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

midwest3 <- mutate(midwest,
  county_type = case_when(
    inmetro == 1 & percbelowpoverty <= 15 ~ "urban-low-poverty",
    inmetro == 1 & percbelowpoverty > 15  ~ "urban-high-poverty",
    inmetro == 0 & percbelowpoverty <= 15 ~ "rural-low-poverty",
    inmetro == 0 & percbelowpoverty > 15  ~ "rural-high-poverty"
  )
)

count(midwest3, county_type)
tasks.test_expression: exists('midwest3') && 'county_type' %in% names(midwest3) && all(c('urban-low-poverty','urban-high-poverty','rural-low-poverty','rural-high-poverty') %in% midwest3$county_type) && all(midwest3$county_type[midwest3$inmetro==1 & midwest3$percbelowpoverty <= 15] == 'urban-low-poverty', na.rm=TRUE) && all(midwest3$county_type[midwest3$inmetro==0 & midwest3$percbelowpoverty > 15] == 'rural-high-poverty', na.rm=TRUE)
hints: Use & to combine two conditions within a single case_when() line: condition1 & condition2 ~ "value".
You need four lines — one for each combination of metro status and poverty level.
inmetro is 0 or 1 (numeric), not TRUE/FALSE, so use == 1 and == 0.
case_when(inmetro == 1 & percbelowpoverty <= 15 ~ "urban-low-poverty", ...)
tasks.ai_context: Key teaching point: combining conditions with & inside case_when(). inmetro is stored as numeric (0/1), not logical, so students must use == 1 not just inmetro on its own. All four combinations are exhaustive so no TRUE catch-all is needed. A common mistake is using | instead of & — encourage students to think through which combinations they want.

tasks.title: Combining recode() and case_when() in one mutate()
tasks.instructions:
You can create multiple columns in a single `mutate()` call — and a later column can use an earlier one computed in the same call.

Using `diamonds`, create two new columns inside one `mutate()`:

**Step 1 — `cut_score`** (use `recode()`):
Convert `cut` to a numeric quality score:
- `"Fair"` → `1`, `"Good"` → `2`, `"Very Good"` → `3`, `"Premium"` → `4`, `"Ideal"` → `5`

**Step 2 — `value_assessment`** (use `case_when()` on `cut_score` and `price`):
- `cut_score >= 4` and `price < 2000` → `"excellent value"`
- `cut_score >= 4` and `price >= 2000` → `"quality piece"`
- `cut_score <= 2` and `price >= 3000` → `"overpriced"`
- Everything else → `"standard"`

Save as `diamonds4` and count the value assessments.
tasks.starter_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

diamonds4 <- mutate(diamonds,

)

count(diamonds4, value_assessment)
tasks.solution_code:
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

diamonds4 <- mutate(diamonds,
  cut_score = recode(as.character(cut),
    "Fair"      = 1L,
    "Good"      = 2L,
    "Very Good" = 3L,
    "Premium"   = 4L,
    "Ideal"     = 5L
  ),
  value_assessment = case_when(
    cut_score >= 4 & price < 2000  ~ "excellent value",
    cut_score >= 4 & price >= 2000 ~ "quality piece",
    cut_score <= 2 & price >= 3000 ~ "overpriced",
    TRUE                            ~ "standard"
  )
)

count(diamonds4, value_assessment)
tasks.test_expression: exists('diamonds4') && all(c('cut_score','value_assessment') %in% names(diamonds4)) && all(c('excellent value','quality piece','overpriced','standard') %in% diamonds4$value_assessment) && all(diamonds4$value_assessment[diamonds4$cut_score >= 4 & diamonds4$price < 2000] == 'excellent value') && all(diamonds4$value_assessment[diamonds4$cut_score <= 2 & diamonds4$price >= 3000] == 'overpriced')
hints: You can create multiple columns in one mutate() call, separated by commas. A column created earlier in the same mutate() can be used in a later one.
For cut_score, use recode(as.character(cut), ...) with integer values: "Fair" = 1L, "Good" = 2L, ...
For value_assessment, use case_when() with & to combine cut_score and price conditions. Don't forget TRUE ~ "standard" as the catch-all.
The five cut levels are: Fair, Good, Very Good, Premium, Ideal — note the space in "Very Good".
tasks.ai_context: Key teaching points: (1) mutate() can create multiple columns at once, and later columns can reference earlier ones from the same mutate() call. (2) recode() can return numeric/integer values, not just character. (3) as.character(cut) is needed because cut is an ordered factor. Common mistake: forgetting the space in 'Very Good', or using == instead of >= for cut_score comparisons. The TRUE ~ 'standard' catch-all is important here since not all combinations are covered by the explicit conditions.
